trajectory_summary: The call opened with a warm, curious customer who arrived with genuine motivation and a concrete workaround idea. Ty disqualified on shade within 90 seconds, then compounded with a second closure on roof-mount limitations, never probing whether the shade assessment was accurate or whether Peter's ground-rack proposal had any routing value. The remaining two minutes were spent on Tesla product education that served Peter's curiosity but produced no appointment value. Ion lost the sit; Peter left satisfied with the information exchange — the exit was clean on his side, but the outcome is a missed opportunity on a motivated, engaged lead.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is listed as 'unknown.' Based on the transcript, the correct tag is no_appointment / early_DQ. The call ended without a booking, a callback, or any handoff path. However, the exit was not a hostile dismissal — Peter closed the call himself on satisfied terms ('That's all, you answered all my questions'). The outcome is a missed sit on a warm lead, not a failed rapport call.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Premature DQ from satellite — Verify must precede disqualification
primary_coaching_focus.quote: I I'm taking a look, and it looks like you have too much shade for us to make our system work anyway. So I'll just get back to us for now.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Ty closed a warm, motivated lead on a remote shade assessment before completing a single required Verify item. Peter had already signaled a prior company interaction, a concrete workaround idea, and genuine bill-pain motivation — all of which Ty never surfaced. The coaching priority is establishing the rule: satellite shade is a flag to probe, not a DQ trigger. Verify completion is what separates a real DQ from a missed sit.

key_moments.quote: That that the the Tesla panels, they that's something only Tesla does. We're a certified Tesla arm installer, but only for their batteries. So that would be something that you would have to look into on. I know that there is, like, a two year back, wait period currently, I believe, with the with the at least their equipment.
key_moments.stewart_read: Peter opened with 'I saw this thing about the Tesla panels' — a direct expectation gap trigger. Ty's reframe is partially correct (Ion is a certified installer, batteries only) but immediately drifts into speculative product education about Tesla's two-year wait period. The codex is explicit: only reframe when the customer raises it, then move to qualify and schedule. Ty reframed correctly but then spent 90 seconds in Tesla product commentary that served no setter purpose and consumed the window where Verify should have started.

key_moments.quote: I talked to somebody about a week or two ago about solar, and it was, like, they wanted, like, 12 panels or something. So I thought this was a different deal.
key_moments.stewart_read: Peter volunteers a direct hit on verify.prior_designs — a required Verify item. He has already had a solar conversation within the past two weeks, received a 12-panel design, and is now calling Ion because he thought it might be a different product. Ty never follows up on this. Who was the prior company? What did Peter think of the design? Was he close to signing? This is competitive context that would have sharpened the pitch and surfaced whether Peter was a comparison shopper or a motivated buyer looking for a reason to move. The 'they didn't wanna hear that' at 01:47 retroactively confirms he was motivated — he had a workaround idea and the prior company dismissed it.

key_moments.quote: I I'm taking a look, and it looks like you have too much shade for us to make our system work anyway. So I'll just get back to us for now.
key_moments.stewart_read: Ty issues a hard DQ on shade from satellite imagery before completing a single required Verify item. No bill, no interest depth, no title check, no prior designs follow-up, no roof age — the call is closed on a remote assessment alone. The shade reading may be accurate, but the DQ is premature: Peter has already signaled a workaround idea (ground racks) and a prior company interaction. A complete Verify sequence would have surfaced whether the shade issue was actually disqualifying or whether there was a routing path.

key_moments.quote: Well, you know, I've said before, if they build racks in the back part of my yard, that would solve the problem. You know? But, they didn't wanna hear that.
key_moments.stewart_read: Peter is not a passive lead — he has already proposed a concrete technical workaround to a prior company and been dismissed. 'They didn't wanna hear that' is a motivated buyer signal: he has thought about this, he has a solution in mind, and he is testing whether Ion will engage differently. This is also a second touch on the prior_designs Verify item — the prior company dismissed his ground-rack idea, which is why he is calling Ion. Ty's response ('my company, we also only do roof mounted systems') confirms the same dismissal pattern and closes the door on any differentiation.
